sets.passage: Last summer, my father took me on my first real camping (1)___, deep into the mountains, far from any town. I had grumbled at first, certain I would be (2)___ without my phone and computer. The first evening, with no signal at all, I felt strangely (3)___, restless and unsure what to do with myself. But as the days passed, something began to (4)___. Without screens to fill every spare moment, I started to truly (5)___ the world around me. I watched the slow drama of a sunset, listened to a river I would once have walked straight (6)___, and lay awake gazing at more stars than I had ever known (7)___. My father and I talked for hours by the fire, sharing stories we had never had the (8)___ to tell at home. With nothing to distract us, we somehow grew (9)___. I learned practical things too — how to pitch a tent, read a (10)___, and cook a simple meal over flames — and each small skill gave me a quiet sense of (11)___. By the end of the week, I was genuinely (12)___ to leave. The trip taught me how much I had been (13)___ by being always connected. We fill our lives with so much noise that we forget how to be (14)___ with ourselves and the people we love. Since then, I make sure to switch everything off now and then, and simply (15)___ where I am.

sets.passage: When my new neighbour Anna moved in, I learned that she was (1)___ and communicated using sign language. At first I felt awkward, unsure how to (2)___ with someone I could not simply talk to. We managed with smiles, gestures and notes, but it felt (3)___, like speaking through a thick wall. One day, I decided I would learn her (4)___. I borrowed books, watched videos, and asked Anna to teach me, which delighted her. Learning to sign was far (5)___ than I had expected; my fingers felt clumsy and I forgot signs constantly. But Anna was a patient (6)___, laughing kindly at my mistakes and celebrating every small (7)___. Slowly, clumsily, I began to be able to (8)___ simple things. The first time we held a real conversation entirely in sign, without paper or guesswork, I felt a joy that is hard to (9)___. A wall between us had quietly come (10)___. Through Anna, I discovered a whole rich (11)___ I had never known existed, with its own jokes, its own poetry, its own pride. I had assumed that her deafness was something to be (12)___; she taught me to see it instead as simply a different way of being in the world. Learning her language did far more than let us (13)___. It made me realise how many people we thoughtlessly shut out, not because we cannot reach them, but because we never (14)___ to try. The effort to understand another person, I learned, is itself a kind of (15)___.

sets.passage: The mathematics exam was the most important of the year, and I was simply not (1)___ for it. The night before, panic had kept me from studying properly, and now, staring at the paper, my mind was nearly (2)___. Two desks ahead sat Daniel, the top student, his answers no doubt perfect. It would have been so easy to (3)___ a glance at his paper. The teacher was looking out of the window; no one would ever (4)___. For one weak moment, I was truly (5)___. A good grade would please my parents and save me from a difficult (6)___ at home. But as I leaned slightly to one side, a voice inside me asked a simple (7)___: if I cheated, what would the grade actually be (8)___? It would not measure my knowledge, only my (9)___. And I would always know that it was not truly (10)___. So I straightened up and turned back to my own paper. I answered what I could and left the rest (11)___, accepting the result I had earned. My grade was poor, and the conversation with my parents was as hard as I had (12)___. But I felt something I had not expected: a quiet (13)___. I had failed the exam, but I had not failed (14)___. Looking back, that low grade matters to no one now. But the choice I made that day, to be honest even when honesty (15)___ me, is part of who I have become.

sets.passage: During my university holidays, I volunteered to teach English for a month at a small school in a poor mountain (1)___. I went, I will admit, partly to help and partly to feel good about (2)___. What I found there changed me far more than I changed it. The school was tiny and (3)___, with broken windows and almost no books. Yet the children's eagerness to learn (4)___ me daily. They walked for hours along mountain paths to reach the school, and their faces lit up at every new (5)___ I taught them. They had so little, and yet complained far (6)___ than the students I knew back in the city. My comfortable assumptions about what people need to be (7)___ were quietly turned upside down. I had arrived imagining myself as the (8)___, the one with knowledge to give. But it was I who received the greater (9)___. The children taught me about gratitude, resilience and joy in (10)___ things. One boy, who owned almost (11)___, shared half his small lunch with me without a second thought, and that generosity humbled me deeply. When the month (12)___ and I had to leave, the children and I both wept. I returned to the city with my view of the world (13)___. I had gone to give a little of my time, but I came back having received a (14)___ I could never repay. Sometimes, I learned, those who appear to have the least are the ones with the most to (15)___.

sets.passage: For most of my life, I had taken my parents almost entirely for (1)___. They cooked, cleaned, worried and worked, and I, like many teenagers, barely (2)___. Their endless small sacrifices were simply the (3)___ I lived in, as invisible to me as the air. Then one day, an English teacher gave us an unusual (4)___: to write a letter of thanks to someone who had shaped our lives, and actually (5)___ it to them. My first thought was that it would feel (6)___. But as I sat down to write to my mother and father, something unexpected (7)___. Forcing myself to put their kindness into words, I began, for the first time, to truly (8)___ it. Memory after memory came back: the meals cooked after long days of work, the worries hidden behind a (9)___, the quiet sacrifices made so that I could have (10)___ they never had. By the time I finished the letter, my eyes were (11)___. When my parents read it, my mother cried, and my usually silent father simply held me, unable to (12)___. We had loved each other for years, of course, but we had rarely said it (13)___. That single letter opened a door between us that had long been closed. I learned that gratitude felt but never (14)___ is a gift left unopened. Since that day, I try never to let the people I love simply (15)___ how much they mean to me.
